tier1_posts.text:
@MitraHispana @nypost Your argument is that the substance (what it's actually doing) doesn't matter and only the form (what it seems to be doing) matters. But I don't see how you can square that with how we look at everything other than AI.

Also, I'd argue that practicing law is actually interpreted

@MitraHispana @nypost How do you know that it makes recommendations and applies reasoning rather than just appearing to do so just as a search engine might to someone who didn't know what the internet was or how a search engine worked?

@MitraHispana @nypost The substance is that AI does not have legal knowledge, does not engage in legal reasoning, and does not make recommendations any more than a search engine does. I think the only way you can say it's practicing law is you look only at the form.

@MitraHispana @nypost It's not though. It's not engaging in legal reasoning. It's not applying legal knowledge. It's not actually recommending anything to anyone any more than a search engine that produces a hit on a page that says "I recommend that you do X" is recommending that the person searching

@love_dem_birds Very unlikely events happen all the time. If you include enough details, every event becomes unbelievably unlikely. I flip a coin and it comes up heads, 50%. But that it took the exact path it did and landed precisely as it did? Astronomically unlikely.

@COOLBREEZE_____ @LundukeJournal It means that whether or not copyright is infringed would be the same analysis whether the image was created by an AI or by a human. The test would be whether the final result contains substantial protected expression found in any prior body of work the process had access to.

@MitraHispana @nypost Practicing law is about the process. If we agree a search engine cannot practice law, then I think you must admit that you cannot tell if law is being practiced just by looking at the input and output. You have to look what is going on internally. Is there reasoning? Judgment?

@MitraHispana @nypost To someone not familiar with how search engines work, a search engine well might appear to exercise judgment or analyze particular facts. But AI does not exercise judgment or analyze particular facts because those are subjective intentional functions. It just appears to.

@MitraHispana @nypost To distill it down, you say, "I have <X> problem with a will" and a person gives you back "see book <Y>". That is definitely not practicing law if the person just knows that book <Y> has to do with wills and ignores the specifics.

But that is practicing law if the person thinks

@MitraHispana @nypost I'm not trying to have anything both ways. As I've consistently said, AI is an inanimate tool that humans put information into and humans use to get information out, like a gun, a book, or a search engine. The law absolutely lets you replace humans if the thing you're replacing

@MitraHispana @nypost It does not make recommendations any more than a book does. It provides text that looks similar to humans providing recommendations, but so does a book. No actual recommending is going on because to "recommend" something is to have a particular intent and mental state.

@MitraHispana @nypost It isn't making recommendations because to "recommend" something is to have a particular subjective intent that an AI cannot have. Again, it's an inanimate tool with prior human design then under its user's control.
@NodrogIce @TheCinesthetic "Why does this keep happening to us?"
@MitraHispana @nypost This is "a gun can commit murder". It's just a category error. AI is an inanimate tool humans use that does precisely what deterministic laws of physics require.

@LundukeJournal The method by which the code is generated only matters if your defense to infringement is lack of access to the original work. Here, their defense would be that insufficient protected expression from the original work appears in theirs. That they used AI to do it is irrelevant.

@MitraHispana @nypost It's not about whether they're "allowed" to, it's about whether they are capable of it. A gun is not capable of murder, so whether it's allowed to murder is a meaningless question. 

And yes, I think AI is an inanimate tool to which concepts like "advice" and "practicing" do not
@Tzn747 @RileeDHarrison California? See my link.

probability.last_reasoning: Bear case probability increased from 10% to 14%: Brent surge to $92.69, two compound stress legs breached, $805B equity wipeout, Fear & Greed at 12 all increase near-term downside risk. XRP at $1.36 is already approaching the <$1 bear threshold. Base case narrowed from 40% to 45% — the $2-8 range remains most likely but the floor is softer. Mid case increased from 38% to 30% — the institutional thesis infrastructure is intact but macro headwinds are stronger. Bull case trimmed slightly from 12% to 11%. The key variable: if oil pushes through $95 and Hormuz escalates, bear case could expand rapidly. Conversely, if Bessent Russia sanctions relief materializes, oil pressure could reverse quickly.

bear_case.score_reasoning: Counter-thesis pressure has increased meaningfully. Macro stress is the primary driver — oil shock, equity selloff, extreme fear — but the fundamental institutional adoption gaps (no ODL data, zero permissioned DEX adoption, ETF AUM data stale) remain unresolved. XRP at $1.36 is 36% above the $1 bear threshold, but a sustained macro deterioration could compress that margin quickly.

competing_infrastructure.name: SWIFT GPI
competing_infrastructure.status_text: SWIFT continues upgrading GPI with pre-validation and tracking. No evidence of XRPL integration or displacement. The 11,500-bank network effect remains SWIFT's strongest moat.

competing_infrastructure.name: Visa B2B Connect
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Visa B2B Connect operates in a parallel lane — focused on card-network-adjacent corridors rather than large-value institutional settlement. Not directly competing for XRPL's target use case.

competing_infrastructure.name: JPMorgan Kinexys
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Kinexys (formerly Onyx/JPM Coin) processes significant daily volumes for JPMorgan clients. It represents a walled-garden approach that could scale without public blockchain infrastructure.

competing_infrastructure.name: BIS Project Nexus
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Nexus remains the highest-credibility competitor — central bank-backed, designed to interlink domestic instant payment systems without private tokens. If Nexus achieves broad adoption, it could eliminate the bridge currency use case entirely.

competing_infrastructure.name: Ethereum Institutional
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Ondo Finance pushing tokenized stocks narrative ($100T+ market). Ethereum's institutional DeFi ecosystem is broader than XRPL's. The 'universal liquidity layer' positioning could go to either chain.

bear_case.odl_stagnation: ODL volume remains the most critical data gap — Ripple stopped publishing data, and without it the $20-30B annualized kill switch is completely unevaluable. This is not neutral; opacity from the primary data source is a bearish signal.
bear_case.token_velocity_concern: No token velocity or average hold time data available. If XRP is being used purely as a bridge with sub-second hold times, price appreciation from utility alone is structurally limited regardless of volume.

macro_headwinds.name: Global Recession Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Oil at $92.69, $805B equity wipeout, gas at $3.45, tariffs active — stagflationary signals are accumulating. A recession would reduce cross-border payment volumes, directly undermining the ODL growth thesis.

macro_headwinds.name: Crypto Winter Signals
macro_headwinds.status_text: Fear & Greed at 12 is extreme but not unprecedented. XRP volume at $1.13B suggests orderly markets, not capitulation. However, sustained macro stress could trigger a broader risk-asset retracement.

macro_headwinds.name: Regulatory Reversal Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Current administration is actively pro-crypto (cyber strategy, CBDC ban push, SEC/CFTC co-location). Regulatory reversal risk is low in the near term but remains a tail risk under future administrations.

macro_headwinds.name: Rate Hike Extension
macro_headwinds.status_text: US 10Y at 4.13% with oil surging to $92.69 creates inflationary pressure that could delay rate cuts or force Fed to hold longer. Higher-for-longer rates reduce speculative capital flows into crypto.

bear_case.bear_narrative: INCREASE MONITORING. Compound stress has escalated to ELEVATED — Brent surged 17.3% to $92.69, now $2.31 from the $95 CRITICAL threshold, while USD/JPY holds at 157.92. The oil leg could breach CRITICAL on a single Iran/Hormuz headline. Three kill-switches escalated to WARNING this cycle: ODL volume is a suspicious absence after three consecutive null cycles, RLUSD tracking is unreliable with a 4.5x discrepancy between aggregate ($1.589B) and XRPL-specific ($355M) supply, and ETF flow monitoring is non-functional. The monitoring framework is losing resolution — the system expresses high confidence across categories while core metrics return null, which per LZ-EPH-002 is a structural error that must be corrected. The thesis paradox holds: a compound stress cascade would simultaneously crush the position and provide the strongest evidence for the use case. I don't know whether the settlement layer is being built behind NDAs or whether it doesn't exist at meaningful scale — and the operator cannot know either until the data opacity resolves or a kill-switch definitively triggers.
